---
title: "Choosing the Cell Resolution"
jupyter: python3
---

```{=html}
<style>
table.dataframe { width: auto; max-width: 90%; font-size: 0.85em; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; }
</style>
```

```{python}
#| label: py-helpers
#| include: false
def _emit(md):
    print("\n" + md + "\n")

def _show_fig(fig):
    _emit('<div style="text-align:center">')
    display(fig)
    _emit("</div>")
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    plt.close(fig)
```

# The resolution problem

The scale-decomposition bound on preferential-sampling bias (Prop.
*scale-decomposition* of the methodology) reads, on an equal-measure partition
$\mathcal{P}_K$ with $K=K_{\mathrm{lon}}K_{\mathrm{lat}}K_t$,
$$
  \big|B_{\mathrm{samp}}\big|
  \;\le\;\sigma_{w^{\star}}\,\sigma_{Y^{\star}}
        \;+\;\sigma_{w^{\perp}}\,\sigma_{Y^{\perp}} ,
$$
with field factors $\sigma_{Y^\star}=\|y^\star\|_{L^2(\nu)}$ (resolved,
between-cell) and $\sigma_{Y^\perp}=\|y^\perp\|_{L^2(\nu)}$ (within-cell), where
$y^\star=\mathsf{P}_K(y-\mu)$, $y^\perp=\mathsf{P}_K^{\perp}(y-\mu)$ are the
cell-averaging / within-cell projections of the OHC field $y\in L^2(\nu)$ and
$\nu$ is the normalized area$\times$time reference measure on
$\mathcal{X}=A\times\mathcal{T}$. The one factor that cannot be estimated from a
sparse point pattern, $\sigma_{w^\perp}$, enters only through the product
$\sigma_{w^\perp}\sigma_{Y^\perp}$; it is therefore bias-irrelevant to the extent
the field is constant within cells. **So the cell scale must make the fraction
of field variance below it, $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$, small** — while
keeping the mean occupancy $N/K$ (in practice $F/K$, with $F$ the number of
distinct floats) above one.

The field factors, unlike $\sigma_{w^\star}$, are computable directly on the
dense GLORYS field over the NAC box (lon $[-68,-62]$, lat $[36,40]$, 2020–2022
daily at $1/12^\circ$), which resolves $\sigma_{Y^\star},\sigma_{Y^\perp},\sigma_Y$
exactly. This report fixes the resolution in two logically separate steps —
**spatial** ($K_{\mathrm{lon}},K_{\mathrm{lat}}$) and **temporal** ($K_t$) —
and, importantly, the spatial choice can be made *independently of seasonality*
(see below), so we settle it first.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| code-summary: "Setup — run (or load cached) R analyses"
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath("../code"))

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import resolution as R

DEPTHS = ["700", "2000"]
D = "../data/leakage"
TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV = f"{D}/leakage_time.csv", f"{D}/leakage_full.csv", f"{D}/leakage_spatial.csv"
VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF = f"{D}/variogram_ranges.csv", f"{D}/variogram_curves.csv", f"{D}/variogram_acf.csv"

if not all(os.path.exists(p) for p in (TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV)):
    R.run_leakage_curves(TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV, depths=DEPTHS)
if not all(os.path.exists(p) for p in (VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF)):
    R.run_variogram(VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF, depths=DEPTHS)

time_df, full_df, spatial_df = R.load_tables(TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV)
vg_ranges, vg_curves, vg_acf = R.load_tables(VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF)
```

# Spatial resolution {#sec-spatial}

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**This section is free of seasonality.** The quantities below are *per-snapshot
spatial* variances. A seasonal cycle that is (near) spatially uniform shifts
every location on a given day by the same amount, and an additive constant does
not change that day's spatial variance. So the spatial within-cell variance —
and hence the choice of $K_{\mathrm{lon}},K_{\mathrm{lat}}$ — is settled on raw
OHC without any deseasonalizing. Seasonality enters only the temporal scale,
handled separately in @sec-temporal.
:::

The most direct test of a spatial grid is empirical: partition each daily
snapshot into $K_{\mathrm{lon}}\times K_{\mathrm{lat}}$ equal-measure
(lon $\times \sin$lat) cells, take the $\cos(\mathrm{lat})$ area-weighted
within-cell spatial variance and total spatial variance, and average over
snapshots (`spatial_leakage` in `code/leakage_curve.R`, eq. *sigmaYperp-hat*
evaluated on the native $1/12^\circ$ grid). Their ratio is the **fraction of
spatial field variance below the cell scale**, $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$ —
exactly the quantity the bound needs small. As cells shrink it must fall to
zero; the question is how small it already is at the coarsest cell the floats
can support, $s_F\approx 76$ km.

```{python}
#| label: spatial-fraction
#| echo: false
_show_fig(R.plot_spatial_fraction(spatial_df, DEPTHS))
```

```{python}
#| label: spatial-table
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
st = spatial_df.copy()
st["depth"] = st["depth"].map(R.DEPTH_LABEL)
st = st[["depth", "target_km", "K_lon", "K_lat", "n_cells", "cell_km", "L", "resid"]]
st = st.rename(columns={"cell_km": "cell (km)", "L": "var frac (perp^2/Y^2)",
                        "resid": "sd ratio (perp/Y)"})
for c in ("cell (km)", "var frac (perp^2/Y^2)", "sd ratio (perp/Y)"):
    st[c] = st[c].round(3)
_emit(st.to_html(index=False, border=0))
```

Reading off the curve at $s_F\approx76$ km fixes the spatial grid: the smallest
$K_{\mathrm{lon}}\times K_{\mathrm{lat}}$ whose cell size sits at or below the
decorrelation scale while keeping the residual fraction acceptably small. The
paper's operating grid $K_{\mathrm{lon}}=6,\ K_{\mathrm{lat}}=5$
($\Delta x\approx88$ km, $\Delta y\approx89$ km) is read directly from this
curve.

## Decorrelation length (directional variogram)

The variogram corroborates the cell-size curve above and, unlike it, exposes the
**anisotropy** that the front imposes. Within each snapshot we form grid-point
pairs (subsampled), classify each by orientation, and accumulate the
semivariance $\tfrac12(y_i-y_j)^2$ into zonal ($|\theta|<30^\circ$), meridional
($|\theta|>60^\circ$) and omnidirectional bins, pooled over snapshots — again a
per-snapshot construction, so again seasonality-free. It *must* be directional:
correlation is long along the current and short across it, and an isotropic
average would hide the short across-front scale, which is the binding one. (Raw
OHC has a per-snapshot planar trend removed so the standing Gulf-Stream gradient
does not render the variogram non-stationary; the deseasonalized panel removes
the per-snapshot mean.)

```{python}
#| label: variograms
#| output: asis
#| echo: false
_emit("::: {.panel-tabset}")
for d in DEPTHS:
    _emit(f"### {R.DEPTH_LABEL[d]}")
    _show_fig(R.plot_variograms(vg_curves, d))
_emit(":::")
```

The binding relevance scale is the **shorter, meridional (across-front)** range:
$\sigma_{Y^\perp}$ is controlled by field variation in *every* within-cell
direction, so the shortest one governs. The methodology's verdict is
$L_{\mathrm{rel}}\gtrsim s_F=\sqrt{|A|/F}\approx76$ km (eq. *verdict*): the
field's shortest decorrelation length must be no finer than the scale the floats
can resolve. Where it holds, cells at the sampling scale sit within a
decorrelation length and $\sigma_{Y^\perp}$ is small; where it fails, a band of
bias-relevant structure is unresolved and the residual term must be carried
rather than assumed away.

```{python}
#| label: ranges-table
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
rt = vg_ranges.copy()
rt["depth"] = rt["depth"].map(R.DEPTH_LABEL)
rt = rt.rename(columns={"range_1e_km": "range 1/e (km)", "range_exp_km": "range exp (km)"})
_emit(rt[["depth", "field", "direction", "sill", "range 1/e (km)", "range exp (km)"]]
      .to_html(index=False, border=0))
```

# Temporal resolution and seasonality {#sec-temporal}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**This section is where seasonality lives.** The number of time bins $K_t$ is
governed by temporal structure of the regional-mean OHC series, which is
dominated by the seasonal cycle; the spatial choice of @sec-spatial does not
depend on any of it.
:::

Collapsing space to the area-weighted regional mean $\bar Y(t)$ (the
$K_{\mathrm{lon}}=K_{\mathrm{lat}}=1$ special case of $\mathcal{P}_K$), we bin
the 2020–2022 series into $K_t$ equal-duration bins and report the fraction of
its temporal variance left within bins, $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$, for the
**raw** series and for a **deseasonalized** series (minus a per-cell,
per-calendar-month GLORYS climatology — an Argo-independent baseline). The gap
between them is the seasonal cycle: the raw fraction stays large until many time
bins resolve the seasonal swing, whereas deseasonalizing removes it up front so
even a coarse $K_t$ leaks little.

```{python}
#| label: temporal-fraction
#| output: asis
#| echo: false
_emit("::: {.panel-tabset}")
for d in DEPTHS:
    _emit(f"## {R.DEPTH_LABEL[d]}")
    _show_fig(R.plot_temporal_fraction(time_df, d))
_emit(":::")
```

The lagged autocorrelation of the deseasonalized regional mean gives the
residual (non-seasonal) temporal decorrelation scale; its $e$-folding lag,
together with the seasonal amplitude above, sets $K_t$.

```{python}
#| label: temporal-acf
#| echo: false
_show_fig(R.plot_temporal_acf(vg_acf, DEPTHS))
```

```{python}
#| label: temporal-table
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
rows = []
for d in DEPTHS:
    for kt in (3, 6, 12, 24):
        f = R.temporal_fraction_at(time_df, d, kt)
        rows.append({"depth": R.DEPTH_LABEL[d], "K_t": kt,
                     "raw": round(f["raw"], 3), "deseas": round(f["deseas"], 3)})
_emit(pd.DataFrame(rows).to_html(index=False, border=0))
```

# Operating partition {#sec-operating}

On the full partition $\mathcal{P}_K$ at the operating spatial grid
($K_{\mathrm{lon}}=6,\ K_{\mathrm{lat}}=5$), the combined field variance splits
by the Pythagorean identity $\sigma_Y^2=\sigma_{Y^\star}^2+\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2$
(eq. *sigmaYK-hat / sigmaYperp-hat*, native grid aggregated to equal-measure
cells). The residual diagnostic there, across $K_t$:

```{python}
#| label: operating-checks
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
gap = R.max_identity_gap(time_df, full_df)
qgap = R.max_quad_gap_frac(full_df)
_emit(f"Pythagorean identity holds by construction (max residual "
      f"${gap:.1e}$); the equal-measure quadrature reconstructs the direct "
      f"$\\cos(\\mathrm{{lat}})$-weighted field variance to within **{qgap:.1%}** "
      f"(largest relative gap across resolutions), bounding the error of "
      f"treating the discrete cells as exactly equal-measure.")

rows = []
for d in DEPTHS:
    for kt in (3, 6, 12, 24):
        sub = full_df[(full_df["depth"] == d) & (full_df["K_t"] == kt)]
        vals = {r["field"]: r["L"] for _, r in sub.iterrows()}
        rows.append({"depth": R.DEPTH_LABEL[d], "K_t": kt,
                     "raw": round(vals["raw"], 3), "deseas": round(vals["deseas"], 3)})
_emit("Combined space$\\times$time variance fraction "
      "$\\sigma_{Y^\\perp}^2/\\sigma_Y^2$:")
_emit(pd.DataFrame(rows).to_html(index=False, border=0))
```
